title: Valuing an Energy Company: Normalize the Cycle, Value the Barrels
quick_definition: Energy valuation is standard valuation with two non-negotiable bends: you must normalize cyclical earnings to a mid-cycle price (never capitalize the spot print), and you must value a depleting asset base (the barrels run out, so no clean perpetuity).
explainer_covers: Duplication guard (what this node does not re-teach). The DCF/WACC/comps/multiples toolkit (★G87–★G95, ★G24–★G27) and ER's process (z3.7/z3.8) are assumed, not repeated. This node adds only the commodity overlay.

The cardinal sin: capitalizing the cycle. Spot earnings at a peak or trough are meaningless for valuation — a cyclical trough EPS looks like distress, a peak EPS looks like a growth stock. You normalize to mid-cycle: run a through-the-cycle price deck (a long-run assumption, not today's tape). This is ★G310, and it's the home the corpus's seven orphaned uses of "normalized earnings" were pointing at.

The depleting-base problem → NAV thinking. A standard DCF assumes a going concern into perpetuity; an oil field depletes. So energy leans on NAV (net asset value) — value the reserves and assets and run them to exhaustion — the direct cousin of ★G235's NAV machinery and real-estate.z5.3's asset-value approach. EV/boe (enterprise value per barrel of reserves) is the quick asset-value yardstick.

The sector's own multiples (Osmundsen). EV/DACF (debt-adjusted cash flow) is the headline energy multiple — it neutralizes the leverage and DD&A-method differences that make P/E incomparable across producers — alongside RoACE (return on average capital employed), the sector's signature return metric.

Growth vs. returns (Antill & Arnott / OIES). Energy's chronic valuation argument: the market rewards capital discipline and returns over production growth — a lesson the sector relearned the hard way after 2014–2020, and the analytical face of the "capital discipline" regime in Z3.2.

Style & rates. Energy is the market's standing deep-value / cyclical exhibit (★G149 + asset-management.z2.5) — low multiples, unloved at the trough, a value-investor hunting ground; and the discount-rate channel (★G259) applies as everywhere.

Three dialects. Hold this node against its two siblings: Financials Z4.1 (the tools partly break), sector-IT Z4.1 (the tools work but inputs mislead), here (the tools work but you normalize the cycle and deplete the base). The contrast is the lesson.

connects_to.note: three valuation dialects — tools partly break
three valuation dialects — tools work but inputs mislead

connects_to_raw: ★G87–★G95 / ★G24–★G27 (the assumed toolkit) · ★G235 / `real-estate.z5.3` (the NAV cousin) · `sector-financials.z4.1` · `sector-information-technology.z4.1` (the three valuation dialects) · ★G149 / `asset-management.z2.5` (the value/cyclical style) · Z3.2 (capital discipline, the valuation regime).
real_world_layer: Osmundsen EV/DACF & RoACE; Antill & Arnott OIES growth-vs-returns; Damodaran cyclical case via ER.

title: Reading the Oil Patch's Books: Reserves Disclosures, DD&A & the Credit Footnote
quick_definition: Energy's financial statements carry disclosures no other sector has — a standardized value of the reserves, depreciation tied to depletion, and accounting-method choices that break comparability — and its credit rests on those same reserves.
explainer_covers: The reserves disclosure (the standardized measure & PV-10). SEC/FASB rules require producers to report a standardized measure of discounted future net cash flows from proved reserves — using prescribed average prices, year-end costs, legislated tax rates (so future income taxes are included), and a 10% discount rate. PV-10 is a closely related non-GAAP figure that starts from the same cash-flow stack but is commonly presented before income taxes. Neither is fair value (the fixed-price rule makes both crude), but the standardized measure is the comparable supplemental anchor analysts start from (COP/XOM, the exhibits) — the core of ★G311.

DD&A on units-of-production. Producers depreciate capitalized oil-and-gas costs as they produce — units-of-production DD&A depends on the depletable cost base, the applicable reserve denominator (typically proved developed reserves), and production, and it moves when costs are added, reserves are revised, properties are acquired, or impairments are taken — the accounting mirror of Z2.1's treadmill.

Successful-efforts vs. full-cost (the comparability trap). Two SEC-permitted methods for capitalizing exploration cost; two otherwise-identical companies can report different earnings and asset bases depending on which they use. The analyst normalizes across the method before comparing — and watches impairments, the write-downs that hit when prices fall.

The upstream scoreboard (the treadmill's report card — folded, not minted). Reserve-replacement ratio (did you replace >100% of what you produced?), finding-&-development (F&D) cost (what a new barrel cost to add), and the recycle ratio (netback ÷ F&D — is finding barrels actually profitable?). Plus hedging disclosures — how much of next year's production is price-locked.

The credit handshake — fixed-income.z4.8 (the third and final leg of FI's deferral). FI's four-part corporate frame was deferred to the sector modules for banks (M8) and for "energy vs. utilities" (named in both z4.8 and z3.7); this closes it. The template applies — but on cyclical inputs. Unlike a bank (where the frame breaks), an energy producer is an ordinary corporate issuer — leverage, coverage, liquidity read normally. The catch is the inputs: run the ratios on mid-cycle (★G310) numbers, because lenders stress a price deck — a credit that's investment-grade at $80 crude is distressed at $40. The 2015–16 and 2020 E&P default waves are ★G255/Z3.2 made real.

One novel structure — reserve-based lending (RBL). Upstream revolvers are sized to a borrowing base = the lender's PV of the borrower's proved reserves (★G305/★G311), redetermined ~semi-annually. When prices crash the borrowing base is cut precisely when the borrower most needs liquidity — a pro-cyclical squeeze (the 2015–16/2020 redetermination crunches). Midstream credit is the opposite temperament — it rides contract quality (take-or-pay counterparties), the direct kinship to private-credit.z2.13.

connects_to.note: which named "energy" in the market's geography
reserves — the asset the books and the loans both rest on
proved reserves — the asset the books and the loans both rest on

connects_to_raw: `fixed-income.z4.8` (the mandatory handshake — FI's deferral completed) · `fixed-income.z3.7` (which named "energy" in the market's geography) · Z2.1 / ★G305 (reserves — the asset the books and the loans both rest on) · ★G310 (mid-cycle inputs) · ★G255 / Z3.2 (the default canon) · `private-credit.z2.13` (midstream's infrastructure-debt lender).

gaps.note: reserves-valuation-source-absent — Antill & Arnott's Valuing Oil and Gas Companies (the reserves-accounting/valuation handbook) was never supplied; PV-10 mechanics, successful-efforts-vs-full-cost nuance, and RBL detail are drafted from the COP/XOM 10-K disclosures, Mu, Osmundsen, the OIES monograph, and architect knowledge. Severity: medium.

real_world_layer: COP/XOM reserve disclosures; Mu; Osmundsen; OIES monograph; RBL borrowing-base mechanics.
